headline: From Hunter to Healer: One Man's Transformation
meta_description: After a bear attack cost him an eye, a hunter in Bangladesh transformed his home into a wildlife rescue center, saving hundreds of animals.
summary_short: A former hunter in northeastern Bangladesh turned his tragedy into purpose, creating one of the country's leading wildlife rescue centers from his home.
body:
A bear attack that cost him an eye became Sitesh Ranjan Deb's unlikely turning point. After three months recovering in the hospital, the hunter from Sreemangal in northeastern Bangladesh began questioning everything he had been taught since childhood—why he hunted, why he killed.

His family had hunted for generations. His father and grandfather were known for tracking leopards and wild boars. But lying in that hospital bed, something shifted. When Deb returned home, he stopped hunting entirely.

Instead, he began rescuing. The grounds of his house became a sanctuary. Jungle-cat cubs, pythons, slow lorises, and injured monkeys occupied bedrooms and courtyards. Two bears he raised, Rambo and Jumbo, ate pumpkins in his yard. A half-dead infant monkey rescued from floodwater was wrapped in warm cloth and fed milk drop by drop.

The center eventually became the Bangladesh Wildlife Service Foundation, one of the country's most respected private wildlife institutions. Deb's years as a hunter—understanding how frightened animals moved, where they hid, how they reacted when cornered—became his greatest asset in rescue work.

He made releases public, inviting officials and villagers to watch cages open. As forests around Sreemangal disappeared, replaced by tourism and settlement, Deb used these moments to explain habitat loss and why animals entered villages.

The work was exhausting and underfunded. But Deb's philosophy remained simple: "You just do it."
